title: Quiz: Fault-Tolerant Compilation
description: Test your understanding of compiling quantum circuits for fault-tolerant execution

questions.question: What is the primary goal of fault-tolerant compilation?

options.text: To minimize the number of qubits
To transform circuits into operations compatible with error correction while preserving correctness
To speed up classical simulation of quantum circuits
To remove all errors from quantum circuits

questions.explanation: Fault-tolerant compilation transforms quantum circuits into sequences of fault-tolerant operations (like Clifford+T) that can be executed on error-corrected qubits while maintaining the computational correctness of the original algorithm.

questions.question: Why is T-count an important metric in fault-tolerant compilation?

options.text: T gates are the fastest gates to execute
T gates require expensive magic state distillation, making them the main resource bottleneck
T-count determines the classical simulation time

questions.explanation: T gates require magic state distillation, which consumes many physical qubits and time. In contrast, Clifford gates are transversal and cheap. Thus, T-count is often the dominant factor in fault-tolerant resource estimates.

questions.question: How does grid-based synthesis (like the Ross-Selinger algorithm) scale for approximating Rz(theta) to precision epsilon?
questions.explanation: Grid-based synthesis algorithms achieve T-count scaling as O(log(1/epsilon)), meaning doubling the precision (halving epsilon) only adds a constant number of T gates.

questions.question: In surface code compilation, how are CNOT gates typically implemented?

options.text: Using lattice surgery with ZZ measurements

questions.explanation: In surface codes, CNOT gates are implemented using lattice surgery, which involves temporarily merging code patches, performing ZZ measurements, and applying corrections based on measurement outcomes.

questions.question: Gate synthesis always produces an exact decomposition of arbitrary rotations into Clifford+T gates.
questions.explanation: False. Most arbitrary rotations cannot be exactly decomposed into Clifford+T. Gate synthesis produces approximate decompositions with error bounded by the specified precision epsilon.

questions.question: What is a common circuit optimization technique specific to fault-tolerant compilation?

options.text: T-T_dag cancellation and phase polynomial optimization

questions.explanation: Common optimizations include canceling adjacent T-T_dag pairs, using T^8 = I identities, and phase polynomial optimization which algebraically simplifies {CNOT, T} subcircuits.

questions.question: What is the standard T-count for decomposing a Toffoli gate into Clifford+T?
questions.explanation: The standard Toffoli decomposition requires 7 T gates. This can be reduced to 4 T gates using ancilla qubits and measurement-based techniques (with potential probabilistic overhead).

questions.question: Which gates are considered 'transversal' (low-cost) in surface code implementations? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: In surface codes, Clifford gates (H, S, X, Y, Z) can be implemented transversally or with low-overhead operations. T gates are NOT transversal and require magic state injection, making them expensive.
